mnemonics.title: Virtual Function Recall
mnemonics.content: VIRTUAL - Vtables Implement Runtime Call Address Lookup: Compiler adds hidden pointer (vptr) to objects, each class gets vtable, function addresses looked up at runtime based on actual object type.

mnemonics.title: Pure Virtual Remember
mnemonics.content: PURE = 0: Think of it as the function having 'zero implementation' - like a placeholder that says 'someone else must fill this in'.

mnemonics.title: Polymorphism Key Points
mnemonics.content: P-O-L-Y-M-O-R-P-H-I-S-M: Pointer/Reference to base holds derived object, Overriding enables different behavior, Late binding resolves at runtime, Your objects become polymorphic, Many forms through inheritance.

acronyms.usage: Runtime data structure storing function pointers for virtual functions, used for dynamic dispatch
Hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable, set up by constructors
C++ mechanism enabling type identification at runtime, used by dynamic_cast and typeid
Idiom to hide implementation details using forward declaration and pointers
C++ idiom where resource acquisition happens in constructor, release in destructor

visualTips.visualization: Think of vtable as a 'function menu' attached to each object. When calling virtual function, program looks at vptr→vtable→finds function address→calls it. Like a restaurant ordering system: customer (caller) → menu (vtable) → kitchen (actual function).

Draw inheritance as family tree. Upcasting = moving UP the tree (safe, like saying 'my child IS-A person'). Downcasting = moving DOWN the tree (risky, like trying to treat any 'person' as your specific child).

Imagine a 'sandwich' where derived class has extra layers on top of base. Assigning to base by value means keeping only the bottom bread, losing the top layers. Pointers/references keep the whole sandwich intact.

Abstract class = 'blueprint' or 'contract'. Think of it like a job interview template - it defines WHAT positions exist (methods) but doesn't hire anyone (no objects). Derived classes must 'fill' these positions.

analogies.analogy: Like a remote control with 'channel' buttons. The button (virtual function) is the same, but pressing it shows different channels (behavior) depending on which TV (object type) it's controlling.

Like a generic 'container' label on a specific box. The label says 'Box' (Base*) but the actual contents could be anything (Derived object). You can only do Box-specific operations unless you know what's inside.
Like a job posting that says 'Position Available - Requirements TBD'. It defines a role that must be filled, but the actual job description comes from whoever takes the position (derived class).
Like a 'kill switch' for a domino effect. Without it, only the top domino falls (base destructor). With it, the entire chain falls properly (derived then base destructors in correct order).
